edges.description: Fibrovascular membrane contraction exerts tractional forces on the retina, causing partial or total detachment.

nodes.description: As the premature infant matures, the incompletely vascularized retina develops increasing metabolic demand. The avascular peripheral retina becomes hypoxic, stabilizing HIF-1alpha and driving strong upregulation of VEGF-A, erythropoietin, IGF-1, and angiopoietin-2. Excess VEGF triggers pathological neovascularization: new vessels grow beyond the inner limiting membrane into the vitreous. These abnormal vessels are fragile and prone to vitreous hemorrhage. Fibrovascular membrane contraction may cause tractional or exudative retinal detachment. The Norrin/FZD4/LRP5 pathway, which normally guides retinal vessel development, is also disrupted in this context.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of HIF-1α in Retinopathy of Prematurity: A Review of Current Literature.
evidence.snippet: the hypoxia-sensitive retina, causing an overproduction of proangiogenic factors and the development of pathological neovascularization

evidence.reference_title: Animal Models of Retinopathy of Prematurity: Advances and Metabolic Regulators.
evidence.snippet: the oxygen-induced retinopathy (OIR) mouse model has gained the most popularity and critically contributed to our current understanding of pathological retinal angiogenesis and the discovery of potential anti-angiogenic therapies

nodes.description: Tractional or exudative retinal detachment occurring in Stage 4 (partial) or Stage 5 (total) ROP. Major cause of blindness in affected infants.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of HIF-1α in Retinopathy of Prematurity: A Review of Current Literature.
evidence.snippet: Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) is a proliferative vascular disease of the retina that poses a significant risk to prematurely born children. If untreated, ROP can lead to retinal detachment, severe visual impairment, and even blindness.
